steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures
steps.content:
To the left of the blank: “Popper's theory” — a noun phrase serving as the subject.

To the right: “that scientific hypotheses must be refutable” (a noun clause), “termed the criterion of falsifiability” (a participial phrase), and then “rejects” governing a direct object.

steps.title: Apply the structures to the choices
steps.content:
The sentence already has one main verb: rejects. That means the blank cannot be another main verb — it must be a participial phrase modifying the subject.
  • Dictating (C) — present participle: "Popper's theory, dictating that…, rejects…" — one main verb (rejects), correct.
  • Was dictating (A) — past progressive main verb: creates two main verbs with no conjunction.
  • Dictates (D) — simple present main verb: same double-verb problem.
  • Dictated (B) — past tense main verb: same double-verb problem (a participial reading of dictated would require a passive structure that doesn't fit here).
Only C keeps rejects as the sole main verb.

That matches choice C — C is correct.
